misconceptionSummary: Students calculate left to right regardless of operation, ignoring priority rules.

lines.text: Worked left to right — ignored multiplication priority

steps.narration: The student worked left to right, subtracting two from twelve before multiplying by three. But multiplication takes priority over subtraction — the multiplication must be done first.

visuals.ruleText: Before calculating, scan the expression for × and ÷ — do those first
visuals.subText: Then return to + and − in left-to-right order

steps.narration: The habit to build: before starting, scan the whole expression for multiplication and division signs. Those operations are calculated first, wherever they appear. Then handle addition and subtraction from left to right.
First, two times three is six. Then twelve minus six is six. The answer is six, not thirty — a very different result from working left to right.
Six. Scan for multiplication and division first, calculate those, then handle addition and subtraction from left to right. Left to right is only correct when all operations have equal priority.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: 12 − 2 × 3 = 30 (worked left to right). Multiplication takes priority — calculate 2 × 3 = 6 first, then 12 − 6 = 6.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Scan for multiplication and division before you start. Those go first, no matter where they appear in the expression. Addition and subtraction wait until all higher-priority operations are done.
